original_abstract: This paper investigates the problem of designing fully distributed consensus protocols for multi-agent systems with general linear dynamics under directed communication topologies. A common limitation in existing consensus literature is the reliance on the smallest real part of the nonzero eigenvalues of the Laplacian matrix—a global parameter that requires agents to know the structure of the entire network. To overcome this, we propose an adaptive consensus protocol that utilizes only the agents' internal dynamics and the relative states of their immediate neighbors. We demonstrate that this protocol achieves leader-follower consensus for any directed graph containing a spanning tree rooted at the leader. By eliminating the need for global graph information, the proposed approach is entirely distributed. Furthermore, we extend the framework to address containment control in scenarios involving multiple leaders.
humanized_abstract: Abstract Consensus protocols for multi-agent systems (MASs) with general linear dynamic modes and directed information exchange topologies are investigated in this paper. Most of the consensus results in the control of MASs are established by using the smallest real part of the nonzero eigenvalues of the Laplacian matrix of the network graph, which is a global quantity that needs global information of the network, and thus is not distributive. In this paper, a distributive adaptive consensus protocol is introduced. The protocol only uses information related to the dynamic modes of individual agents and their neighbors' states and as a result, leader-follower consensus can be established for any directed graph having a spanning tree from the leader to other followers. Extensions to the multi-leader containment control are then developed.

In this paper, a distributive adaptive consensus protocol is introduced. The protocol only uses information related to the dynamic modes of individual agents and their neighbors' states and as a result, leader-follower consensus can be established for any directed graph having a spanning tree from the leader to other followers. Extensions to the multi-leader containment control are then developed.
